examples.title: Complete CUDA Vector Addition Program
examples.description: Full working CUDA program demonstrating all essential steps for vector addition on GPU
examples.explanation: This complete CUDA program demonstrates: (1) Host/device memory allocation using malloc and cudaMalloc, (2) Data transfer via cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyHostToDevice direction, (3) Kernel launch with calculated grid size ensuring coverage of all n elements, (4) Synchronization with cudaDeviceSynchronize(), (5) Result retrieval via cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost, and (6) Proper memory cleanup with cudaFree and free.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - each element processed once by one thread
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - three arrays of n floats on device plus three on host

examples.title: Vector Addition with Stride Pattern for Large Vectors
examples.description: Kernel demonstrating stride pattern to handle vectors larger than total thread count
examples.explanation: The stride pattern allows handling vectors of any size with a fixed grid. Total threads = blockDim × gridDim. Each thread processes elements at indices: idx, idx+stride, idx+2*stride, etc. This ensures complete coverage even when n >> total_threads. For n=10M and 16K threads, each thread processes ~610 elements on average.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n/t) where t is total thread count, O(n) total work

examples.title: 2D Vector Addition Using 2D Thread Organization
examples.description: Demonstrates mapping 2D threads to 2D array elements for matrix-style vector operations
examples.explanation: This code extends 1D vector addition to 2D matrix addition using dim3 type for blocks and grid. Each thread is identified by (blockIdx.x, blockIdx.y) and (threadIdx.x, threadIdx.y), mapped to 2D array indices. This is useful for image processing, linear algebra operations on matrices, and any 2D data structure.
examples.timeComplexity: O(ROWS × COLS) - each element computed once
examples.spaceComplexity: O(ROWS × COLS) for three matrices on device
